coredata.dc:description: Inorganic and organic nanoparticles are used for abundant number of applications due to their novelty, surface charge, alloys, and oxides. Among the different types of nanoparticles, carbon nanomaterials (CNM) are gaining constant attention for biomedical applications. Their unique physical and chemical characteristics, like electrical, thermal, structural, and optical diversity, make them suitable materials. Selecting CNM for the treatment and diagnosis of disease and infection will provide controlled, predictable, low cytotoxic, and target-specific delivery of drugs for biomedical applications. Different types of CNM, like graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes, nanorods, nanowires, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), need to be investigated for application in Cancer treatment. Therefore, this review focuses on the different types of synthesis, drug loading, surface coating, and application of CNM as a drug delivery agent for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Inorganic and organic nanoparticles are used for abundant number of applications due to their novelty, surface charge, alloys, and oxides. Among the different types of nanoparticles, carbon nanomaterials (CNM) are gaining constant attention for biomedical applications. Their unique physical and chemical characteristics, like electrical, thermal, structural, and optical diversity, make them suitable materials. Selecting CNM for the treatment and diagnosis of disease and infection will provide controlled, predictable, low cytotoxic, and target-specific delivery of drugs for biomedical applications. Different types of CNM, like graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotubes, nanorods, nanowires, and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), need to be investigated for application in Cancer treatment. Therefore, this review focuses on the different types of synthesis, drug loading, surface coating, and application of CNM as a drug delivery agent for cancer diagnosis and treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology is blending with different areas of science, including particle engineering, synthesis chemistry, material engineering, and biomedicine. In recent times, nanotechnology has influenced biomedicine by revolutionizing drug delivery, diagnostic tools, and therapeutics to treat infections and diseases [1]. Synthesis of particles between size range of 1–100 nm in the different shapes (sphere, cubes, capsules, clove shaped) [2–6] and materials (polymers, lipids, metals, metal oxides) [5,7,8] has been applied for biomedical application. But among the different material, carbon-based materials are meagrely used for biomedical application. Carbon nanomaterials (CNM) are interesting innovation of nanoscience gaining constant attention in science and technology [9,10]. The diverse range of CNM like carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene quantum dots (GQD), graphene oxide (GO), fullerene, Carbon nanofibers (CNF) and carbon sheets (CS) make them easily explorable for biomedical applications [11,12]. Sumio Iijima in 1991 observed the multiwall CNT formation from the carbon arc discharge and later Sumio Iijima and Donald Bethune produced single wall CNT [13,14]. The nanoscale dimensions of CNMs like graphene, fullerences, carbon fibers, amorphous carbon and carbon nano onions represents alternative structure, and they are allotropes of one element of carbon [15]. Carbon nanotubes and carbon fibers are 1D structure and graphene is 2D structured nanomaterial [11,16]. Studies suggests the application of CNM for various biological applications like drug delivery, biosensors, cancer therapy, tissue engineering, diagnosis of disease and infection and imaging for diagnosis purposes [11,17–19]. The toxicity of CNMs remains a concern for their application for biomedical purposes.

CNMs toxicity is due various factors like shape, size, chemical ratio, also it differs based on the intake, cellular uptake, interfering in the mechanism of cytoplasm and nucleus. In certain cases, adulteration of CNMs is possible making the materials toxic [11]. While synthesis of CNMs, mixing with metal ions will increase the cytotoxicity to live cells. Studies suggests the uptake of CNMs damages the DNA and lipids in cancer cells by stimulating reactive oxygen species (ROS) leading to death of the cancer cells. Also, graphene materials can increase the ROS will affect macrophage activity and cause mitochondrial membrane damage and causes apoptosis of the cells [20,21].

Altering the surface functionalization of CNMs can improve the solubility, safety, and biodegradability. This will also improve the drug delivery efficiency by low clearance and long-term retention of the drug in the body [11]. Cationic and anionic functionalization can increase the toxicity more than non-functionalized CNMs [11,20]. Reports suggest that surface functionalization of CNMs can make them more dynamic by surface tagging of amino acids, drugs, small molecules, peptides, and protein and will also enhance the solubility and efficiency of the CNMs [21]. Tagging of target specific molecules on CNMs is done using different interaction techniques like ionic, covalent and vander Waal forces [22–24]. These modification leads to the inheritance of extensive characteristics and properties for biomedical application. The interaction of active molecules or drugs helps in modify the surface functional groups for drug loading and grafting desired shapes of CNMs. Therefore, exploring the unique characteristics of CNM to improve the physical, chemical, and biological properties for biomedical application is the current necessity [21–24]. Embracing the application of CNMs for target specific drug delivery, biosensing, bioimaging, photodynamic therapy of cancer cells will be the focus of the present review.

Cancer is a becoming a global concern and seriously increasing the morbidity and mortality rate. It is disease condition due to uncontrolled, invasive and random division of cells inside the host organs. The most influential and specific reason associate with the development of cancer is environment (exposure to radiation and pollution) and unhealthy lifestyles (smoking, tobacco consumption, lack of physical activity, imbalanced diet and stress). But only 5–10 % of cancer is associated with inherited genetics involving the mutation of protooncogenes, genes for DNA repair and suppressing of gene expression [25,26]. The frontliners are struggling in finding more specific, safer, and efficient treatment methods to fight against cancer. In USA alone, about 1,898160 new cases and 608,570 deaths were reported due to cancer in 2021. There were decline in cancer cases due to the limitation of using alcohol and tobacco in several countries, but the rise of COVID-19 in 2020 increased the cases again due to the lack of treatment and facilities available for cancer patients [27]. Cancer has been treated and diagnosed using conventional therapies like chemotherapy, surgical removal of tumours, radiation, target specific therapy, hormone therapy and immunotherapy [28,29]. Majorly used therapies like chemotherapy and radiation therapy involves in cytotoxicity and cytostatis of tumor cells, but still, they have side effect, and the risk of recurrences is very high. Conventional radiation and chemotherapy have side effects like bone marrow suppression, neuropathies, skin disorders, hair fall, fatigue, and gastrointestinal issues. Some anticancer drugs like bleomycin and anthracyclines can also cause pulmonary and cardio toxicity [30]. To overcome these problems with conventional method of cancer treatment and diagnosis, nanomedicine has been tested in efficient diagnosis, monitor and treatment of cancer under controlled condition [8]. Metallic and non-metallic nanoparticles are known for several biomedical application like cancer cell killing [7,31,32], antibacterial agents [33], antibiofilm agents [2,3,6] and so on. The National Institute of Health has referred application of nanotechnology can improve the target specificity and increase the efficacy of the treatment. Among the different nanoparticles, Carbon nanomaterials are potential drug delivery agents, they serve as a vehicle for the delivery of anticancer drugs to the cells site. In addition, the pharmacological activity of the drug is also improved by controlled drug release, target specific delivery and prolonged delivery of the drug to the cancer cells. Therefore, CNMs specifically graphene, graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes and carbon quantum dots can easily replace and correct the defect and side effects of convention cancer treatment methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Different types of carbon nanomaterials used for biomedical applications
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene was first synthesized and characterized by Geim and Novoselov in 2004 and awarded with a Nobel Prize in Physics in 2010 [34]. It is structurally, layered and densely packed sp2 carbon atom with hexagonal arrangements of the materials. Chemically and structurally graphene is in bulk structure with relatively large surface area, dispersible in polar and non-polar solvents. They have unique optical property with high thermal and electrical conductivity, also the atoms in the structural surface of graphene permits positions for the attachment of various other biomaterials. The application of graphene is done for drug delivery, biosensors, genetic engineering, intracellular nanocarrier for synthetic ssDNA, proinflammatory therapeutic tools and bioimaging for diagnosis [35,36]. The diverse range of CNM like carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene quantum dots (GQD), graphene oxide (GO), fullerene, Carbon nanofibers (CNF) and carbon sheets (CS) make them easily explorable for biomedical applications ( Fig. 1) [11,12].

CNTs are cylindrical large molecules in hexagonal structure of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [37]. Further research on CNT revealed the formation of hollow cylinder with graphene sheets are known as single walled CNT (made with rolling single graphitic sheet) and multiwall CNT (made with multiple graphitic sheets leaving interlayer spacing between each sheet [14]. CNTs are extremely strong and reliable due to flexibility and stretchability, thermal and electrical conductivity. CNTs are used for real time monitoring and efficient tracking of drug delivery due to its intrinsic spectroscopic characteristics (Raman and Photoluminescence) [35]. The drawbacks of CNTs are the less numbers of techniques or methods for the mass production of CNT synthesis.

In 1985, a new carbon allotrope named fullerenes were first discovered, later Nobel prize for Chemistry in 1996 was awarded for the invention. Fullerenes are revolutionary invention with sp-2 carbon atom cages of different sizes with single or double bonds [15,38]. Fullerenes have highly symmetric carbon nanostructure with high structural and chemical stability. Fullerenes have various photoelectrochemical properties making them reliable for photodynamic therapy [39]. They are low soluble and hydrophobic material applicable for biomedical purposes. The structural surface of fullerene is decorated for various application like diagnosis, imaging, biosensing and target specific drug delivery for disease and infection [40,41].

In the structure of graphene sheets, the carbon atoms are arranged in hexagonal ring shape. The layers of graphene sheets are linked with strong covalent bond and atoms in graphene sheets are sp2 hybridized. It is a very light weight (0.77 mgm−2) and strongest material with tensile strength of 1.3 × 10 PA. Graphene molecules have a repeating sp2 hybridized backbone making them flexible, rigid, and stable molecule. It has a delocalized pi electron system on the surface making the fluid movement of electrons. The electron does not need input energy in the system due to the overlapped pi electrons with no band gap. Graphene can be synthesized in different forms like graphene nanoplatelets, graphene oxide, graphene quantum dots and graphene nanoribbons. Graphene nanoribbons (GNR) are single layer of narrow lengthened strips having high surface area, unique thermal, mechanical, electronic, and optical property [42,43]. GNR are non-toxic to mammalian cells and environment, therefore they can be used for biomedical application without non-covalent or covalent modification [44,45]. Graphene nanoflakes (GNF) are 30 nm (diameter) size graphene sheet with pristine aromatic system and COOH group terminated edges. The size and degree of oxidation affects the structural and dynamic properties of GNR. The rough and curvature structure of GNR does not influence the water dipole orientation of the surface [46].

Nanomaterials ranging between the size of 2 – 50 nm are Quantum dots (QDs). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are carbon nanoparticles with physical, chemical, and biological applicable for biomedical purposes [47]. They are colloidal semiconductor nanocrystals with quantum confinement characteristics possessing fluorescence emission to visible to infrared (IR) wavelength on excitation [47]. They have tuneable emission spectra, resistance to photo bleaching and high brightness. The specific structure of GQDs makes them suitable for application as loading of small molecules for drug delivery, fluorescence bioimaging, biosensing and killing of cancer cell using photothermal and photodynamic therapy [48,49].

Carbon nanodots (CDs) are a new type of nanocarbon that possesses several advantages over carbon nanotubes [50]. Simple hydrothermal treatment can be used to synthesize CNDs from a variety of biological resources [51]. MWCNTs can be oxidized and exfoliated to produce graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [52]. The chemical exfoliation of graphite nanoparticles was used by Liu et al. [53] to create highly homogeneous GQDs. It is possible to prepare N-doped GQDs in the presence of urea by paralyzing citric acid.

Carbon nano horns (SWNHs) are like single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs); however, SWNHs are formed by rolling graphene sheets into cones rather than tubes [54]. Individual SWNHs assemble into spherical structures due to van der Waals forces between their open ends [55]. In intravenous injections, SWNH aggregates are about 80–100 nm in size, which is optimal for the EPR effect, enabling them to accumulate at the tumor site [56]. SWNHs are also synthesized without the use of metal catalysts. As a result, they have a reduced cytotoxic effect. Due to their shape, the endocytosis of SWNHs requires less energy than carbon nanotubes. SWNHs also have a larger surface area and internal storage space than carbon nanotubes. Sonicating pristine SWNHs can prepare SWNHs with poly [maleic anhydride-alt-1-octadecene-poly (ethylene glycol)] (C18PMH-PEG) for 1 h; then ultrafiltration purifies the resultant solution [54,55].

Graphene sheets are stacked vertically on a substrate to form carbon nano walls (CNWs) [56]. As a result of photocatalytic degradation of graphene oxide nanoplatelets with TiO2 nanoparticles, reduced graphene oxide nano mesh (RGONM) is formed. The aspect ratio (length to diameter), specific surface area, and electrical properties of these materials are excellent. In addition to being approximately 61 nm in diameter, the graphene oxide nano meshes are about 0.9 nm thick, and the average size of pores was about 8 nm [57]. The NIR absorption of these nanoplatelets is much higher than that of graphene oxide nanoplatelets, so they are very suitable for photothermal therapy.

Nanodiamond particles (ND) exhibit great potential due to their shape and small size (from 4 to 10 nm in diameter), large surface area, and carbon sp2/sp3 hybridized bond content ratio [58]. NDs in anticancer therapy are one of their many biomedical applications. The delivery of anticancer drugs (drug delivery systems, DDSs) through these nanoparticles has been the subject of many recent studies. In addition to their high biocompatibility, NDs allow the drug to be delivered to a specific site, enhancing its bioavailability [59]. Several methods can synthesize NDs, but the detonation technique is the most common and cost-effective method [60,61].

Carbon onions are composed of spherical carbon shells resembling onion layers and have a concentric layered structure [61,62]. It is sometimes called onion-like carbon (OLC) or carbon nano-onions (CNOs). This includes nested fullerenes, small (<100 nm) polyhedral nanostructures and ultrasmall (80 nm) nanoshells [62]. Many different methods can synthesize OLC; inert gas is used for annealing and transforming NDs to OLC [63]. Graphite electrodes in water and CVD are examples of another synthesis technique [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanomaterials synthesis and growth methods
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of CNMs is a complicated process involving various factors like catalyst, temperature, precursors, and conditions. Significant improvement and progress have made CNMs available for commercial production. Each CNMs can be produced or synthesized using different techniques, likewise the growth condition also differs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods for the synthesis of graphene nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is synthesized using three different methods namely chemical vapor deposition, chemical or plasma exfoliation and mechanical cleavage from natural graphite. But these methods are not useful for commercial production of graphene. Chemical vapor deposition (CVD) is the most common method for the synthesis or production of graphene. CVDs occurs in reaction chamber, where volatile gas molecules are combined and deposited on a substrate. The residual gas is pumped out after formation of the material on the substrate surface. Temperature plays major role in CVDs reaction, also graphene produced using CVDs methods a high pure quality, but toxic by-products are released due to the use of volatile precursor gas. Formation of graphene film occurs in two steps; they are pyrolysis of the precursor material to form carbon materials on a substrate materials and heat intensive step of assembling the dissociated carbon atom over a substrate to form a single structural layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods for the synthesis of carbon nanotubes
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanotubes are simple materials synthesized using methods like Chemical vapor deposition, discharge, electrolysis, arc discharge, hydrothermal and template directed synthesis and laser ablation ( Fig. 2).

The common parameters used for CNTs synthesis are metal catalyst, temperature, and carbon feedstock. CNMs like single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) are synthesized using laser ablation and template/bottom-up methods, while multi walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) are synthesized using chemical vapor deposition, hydrothermal and electrolysis methods. But still there are no available synthesis method capable of synthesizing commercial grade of single walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) and multi walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). In recent times fast and cheaper CNTs synthesis methods using microwave oven has been used to synthesis MWCNTs using complex metal, mixture of metals, nanofiber, and nickel nanoparticles. The growth of CNTs can be done using chemical vapor deposition, arc discharge and laser ablation. All these growth mechanisms are like the synthesis mechanism. The common methods used for the growth of CNTs are tip growth model and base growth model. The growth of CNTs is performed in three main steps,
•
The catalyst surface was supplied with carbon feedstocks to produce fullerenes as intermediate
•
Scoot (small carbon fragments like C2, C3) is generated from the decomposition of hydrocarbons by heat and subsequently is deposited on catalysts surface
•
Finally, nanotube grows from the deposited metastable carbide particles until the catalysts and carbon fragments are saturated.
There are still controversies and difficulties in finding a suitable and cheaper technique for the growth of CNTs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be synthesized using top down and bottom methods [64]. The bottom-up method is the most common and preferred synthesis method due to the synthesis of controlled size CQDs [65–67]. The size of the GQDs can also be controlled by opening the cage of fullerene and using bottom-up method. Under stringed growth condition and using very specific types of organic material undergoing complicated reaction steps can be used for the synthesis of GQDs [65–67]. But synthesis of GQDs using top-down method involves the cutting of bulk size of carbonaceous material into smaller pieces. The starting material for GQDs synthesis using top-down method are abundant carbon materials. It is used to produce easy and bulk mass of GQDs using cheap carbon materials. Graphene oxide is the suitable due to the presence of abundant oxygen in their functional group. This makes them easily breakable in smaller nano-meter size and single layered GQDs [68,69]. In general, the bulk material used of the synthesis of GQDs involve Graphene, Graphene oxide, Carbon nanotubes, carbon nanofibers and Graphite. Graphene oxide nanosheet are simpler to break due to the presence of oxygen in the functional group. Methods like hydrothermal (temperature treatment at 200 °C), solvothermal (different solvents treatment), ultrasonication (ultrasonication in the presence of strong acidic condition), microwave (uses the heat of microwave) and radical methods (generates fluorescents carbon materials from the graphite electrode by electrochemical methods) are used to breakdown graphene nanosheets into GQDs ( Fig. 3). But to improve the yield of GQDs using top-down method various techniques like including strong acidic condition (modified hummer method) and using microwave. But separation of GQDs with same diameter using top-down method is marginally successful, but still the synthesizing-controlled size GQDs was not greatly successful.

The synthesis of GQDs using CNTs and carbon fibres is done unzipping the longitudinal graphene nanoribbons. The backbone of the heteroatom doped CNTs, and carbon ribbon’s structure was broken into nanosized carbon materials and exfoliated into GQDs. Under vacuum condition the potassium ions in the nanosized carbon are intercalated into layered structure. This layer structure is exposed to air, ethanol, and water to produce 22.96 % and 9.9 % of CNTs and carbon fibres respectively.

GQDs from graphite can be synthesized using Hummer’s method and acid free methos. GQDs can be converted from graphite by repeated oxidation and reduction reaction. In the hummer’s method, the GQDs are prepared by oxidizing the graphite and cutting them into smaller size. The preparation condition of GQDs using hummer’s method is stringent than cutting of bulk size graphite materials. In the process of GQDs synthesis, the graphite material is oxidized in the presence of higher concentration and volume of H2SO4 and NaNO3. The treated graphite sheets are heated at higher temperature under higher pressure inside an autoclave vessel to cleave them into smaller nano sized GQDs. The yield produced is around 63 % with uniform size of multilayer or monolayers of GQDs. Still this process is not applicable to produce bulk amount of GQDs.

The removal of strong acid from the GQDs synthesized using hummer’s method is the major drawback. To avoid the usage of acids for the synthesis of GQDs, researchers have used graphite intercalation compounds and oxone (monosulphate compounds) to produce GQDs. In mass production of GQDs with exfoliation of graphite intercalation compounds, potassium sodium tartrate was used as an important reagent to convert graphite into graphite intercalation compounds by minimum damage [70]. Therefore, usage of solvent instead of acids increases the yield and avoids the removal of acids. In another method, usage of Oxone a monosulphate compound can be used to neutralize GQDs. Treatment of graphite using sono and photooxidation method in the presence of graphite produces GQDs. Thermal treatment can also be used instead of sono and photo oxidation treatment methods [71]. Therefore, mixing graphite with oxone produces acid free and high yield GQDs, but the yield is not higher as hummer’s method or acid treatment method.

Biocompatibility and water dispersibility of nanomaterials are important considerations for living organisms [61]. Carbon nanomaterials such as fullerene, CNTs, CNHs, and graphene are insoluble in aqueous solutions because of their hydrophobic interactions and π–π interactions, which makes them less effective for biological applications [61,72]. Various strategies have been proposed to disperse carbon nanomaterials in an aqueous solution and make them biologically compatible, including covalent and non-covalent surface functionalization [73]. Two main approaches have been proposed for distributing carbon nanomaterials in aqueous solutions and making them biologically compatible: covalent and non-covalent surface functionalization [72].

Covalent functionalization usually involves the introduction of reactive groups (e.g., -OH groups, -COOH groups, -NH2 groups) onto the surface of pristine carbon nanomaterials, generally concerning fullerenes, CNTs, CNHs, and graphene [61,72]. The introduced functional groups offer huge possibilities for further conjugation with other functional molecules to improve biocompatibility and endow certain functions to the as-prepared carbon nanomaterials [55,61]. Carbon nanomaterials can be covalently functionalized with oxidizing agents to form -COOH groups. These carbon nanomaterials can be further covalently conjugated to other functional molecules such as hydrophilic polymers (e.g., polyethylene glycol (PEG), poly(ethylene imine) (PEI), or biomacromolecules (e.g., DNA, protein), targeted ligands, or therapeutic agents via amidation reactions after the production of -COOH groups [61]. Carbon nanomaterials possess significant delocalized π–π systems that are responsible for their exceptional electronic properties and their strong interactions with low-frequency photons from infrared to terahertz, conferring them with excellent NIR-mediated photothermal conversion capabilities. A covalent surface functionalization of carbon nanomaterials may damage the delocalized π–π networks, thereby affecting photothermal conversion capability [55,61].

A non-covalent functionalization is relatively mild compared to covalent functionalization since their π–π networks aren't perturbed. Hydrophobic motifs can be noncovalently anchored to hydrophilic or bioactive molecules on pristine carbon nanomaterials [55,61]. Aqueous solution stabilizes the nanomaterial by extending the hydrophilic ends into it. Surfactants, polymers, DNA, proteins, and viruses can be used to wrap carbon nanomaterials to achieve noncovalent surface functionalization [55,61]. Several noncovalent interactions can be used to functionalize carbon nanomaterials, including hydrophobic interactions, π–π interactions, and electrostatic interactions. Carbon nanomaterials can be effectively stacked by water-soluble aromatic molecules such as pyrene, porphyrins, and phthalocyanines. The stabilization of carbon nanomaterials has also been achieved by using biomacromolecules, including DNA and proteins. Noncovalent surface functionalization has its defects as well as covalent surface functionalization. Nanohybrids have low stability despite noncovalent functionalization not disrupting the carbon nanomaterials π–π networks. An appropriate functionalization method must be considered carefully to design a phototherapeutic system based on carbon nanomaterials. Biological applications need to maintain a balance between structural integrity and stability.

CNMs are ideal delivery vehicle for targeting therapeutic molecules to the action site without degrading the molecules loaded in them. It also helps in avoiding dilution, reducing the off-target effect, and increasing the efficacy of the molecule on the site of action [74]. Different types of CNMs can efficiently reduce the growth of cancer cells with effective and sustained anti-cancer drug release ( Fig. 4). Several anticancer drugs have been loaded onto CNMs through appropriate functionalization, including doxorubicin (DOX), betulinic acid (BA), MTX, gemcitabine (GEM), etoposide, paclitaxel (PTX), chelerythrine, camptothecin (CPT), carboplatin, cisplatin (CIS), platinum (II), and platinum (IV) [61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Graphene and graphene oxide (GO) nanomaterials for cancer diagnosis and treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Among the different CNMs, graphene nanomaterial (GNMs) is the first choice for therapeutic application due to its low toxicity, high flexibility, and ability of the surface electron to be modified for variety of applications. Drug delivery for site specific action is the first and foremost application of GNMs. Graphene sheet interacting drugs having aromatic or hydrophobic regions were initially used selected for loading of GNMs [75,76]. Doxorubicin can bind to graphene by employing hydrogen bond to oxygenated functional groups permitting controlled release of the drug, while SN38 can be attached by hydrophobic interaction and π-π stacking to the GNMs [77,78]. Genetic material can also be loaded on the graphene by adsorption through interaction of the nucleic acid base with polyaromatic basal plane of GNMs. This allows the delivery of single stranded nucleic acid without degradation due to temperature, pH and enzymes [79–82]. Superparamagnetic FeO reduced GO nanohybrid synthesized using one step co precipitation methods were loaded with DOX and tested against human cervical cancer (HeLa cells). In the presence of magnetic field (AC field-335 Oe; fixed frequency of 265 kHz) for 35 min, GO nanohybrid showed 90 % of cancer cell proliferation at the concentration of 2 mg/mL [83]. Two nanocomplexes namely MUC1 aptamer-NAS-24 aptamer-GO and MUC1 aptamer-Cytochrome C aptamer-GO exhibited programmed cell death of MDA-MB231 and MCF-7 breast cancer cell line [84]. Hybrid nanocomposite of GO and FeO showed 70 % cell viability under in vitro condition, while exhibited 80.5 % of A549 human lung epithelial adenocarcinoma cell at 41–60 min using radio frequency hyperthermia [85]. Chitosan conjugated GO showed approximately 65 % of DOX entrapping with approximately 50 % of drug release within 48 h of time. DOX-Chitosan conjugated GO showed intracellular delivery of DOX in PC3 prostate cancer cells [86]. Wang et al. [87] showed reduction of tumour in mice model using PEGylated nanocomposite with reduced GO under photothermal irradiation (805 nm laser). GO nanocomposite conjugated with chitosan/poly (lactic acid) (PLA) and loaded with DOX showed controlled release of drug in the nanofibrous scaffolds. The release of DOX from the nanofibrous scaffolds were higher under acidic pH 5.3 and showed higher proliferation of lung cancer cells in the presence of magnetic field [88]. In a recent study cisplatin and DOX were loaded in PEGylated GO nanoparticles showed efficient chemotherapy in a single system. The GO conjugate showed prominent death of cancer cell and showed 18.6 % of necrosis rate under in vivo condition [89]. GO functionalized with folic acid and loaded with DOX showed more than 50% of reduction in cancer cell growth at 3 µg/mL concentration [90]. Using carbon nanotubes (CNTs) as support for single-site Fe-N-C catalysts (CNT/Fe-N-C), this catalyst can exhibit peroxidase (POD)-like activity. The breast cancer cell membrane (CM) was targeted with mesoporous CNTs/Fe-N-C wrapped around doxorubicin (DOX) [91]. Because of its efficient catalytic ability, increased drug release rates, and significant photothermal effects, the ultimate nano agent (CNTs/Fe-N-C/DOX/CM) showed high antitumor activity [91]. Hybrid of GO and Au nanoparticles were used for multimodal imaging of cancer cells and showed selective killing of cancer cells under in vitro and in vivo condition [92].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanotubes for cancer diagnosis and treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) are easily engulfed or taken by live cells, CNTs in needle shape can easily and efficiently penetrate the membrane of cells needed for medical application. The cellular uptake mechanism depends on the size, length, surface functionality and degree of aggregation of CNTs [93]. Modifying the surface, changing the specificity, and increasing the cell targeting ability will solve the concerns about the immune response of pulmonary system against pristine CNTs and changes in the gene expression occurring due to the exposure to CNTs. Changing or adding surface functionalities on the CNTs will increase the cellular uptake efficacy via enhanced permeability and retention. This mechanism cause EPR effect leads to accumulate nanoparticles in the tumour cells due to increase in blood flow and targets the selective aptamers or antigens [94]. CNTs are used for delivering anticancer drugs to the tumour site, the anti-cancer drug can be loaded or bind to tips and walls of CNTs and in some cases the drugs are functionalized on the external wall of CNTs [95] ( Fig. 5).

Functionalized CNTs can transversely cell membranes by energy independent pathways. Functionalized CNTs can be attached with ligands detecting cancer specific receptors in the cancer cell surface, this will help in effective and selective anticancer drug delivery to the tumor cells [96–98]. CNTs can be integrated with small molecules, polypeptides and nucleonic acids due to their specialized and specific ultra-high surface area. The presence of endocytosis and phagocytosis mechanisms, CNTs can easily penetrate mammalian cell membrane [99]. In a study by Huang et al. [100], CNTs-DOX were conjugated inside a folic acid conjugated chitosan material to increase the stability of the drug. In another study, Hyaluronan and DOX were loaded within CNTs. Under in vivo condition, CNTs loaded with hyaluronan and DOX were tested in mice with tumor cells, because of the treatment showed prolonged life and improved the condition of mice [101]. PEG-SWCNTs were loaded 5-fluorouracil and delivered into MCF-7 human breast cancer cells under in vitro condition showed successful killing of cancer cells [102]. In another study, DOX at higher concentration were loaded on PEG-MWCNTs and targeted the ligand estrone on human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) [103]. SWCNTs were functionalized using carboxylation, acylation, amination, PEGylation and conjugated with gemcitabine. About 43.14 % (w/w) of Gemcitabine was conjugated on SWCNTs and 37.32 % (w/w) of Gemcitabine was conjugated on SWCNT-PEG. The growth of tumour was suppressed by gemcitabine conjugated with SWCNT-PEG in B6 nude mice [104]. CNTs (CNTs-1, CNTs-2, CNTs-3 and CNTs-4) in different length and diameter were synthesized showing 5–10 % adherence to the urothelium of mouse bladders in ex vivo condition using Franz diffusion cells. This suggested that shorter size of CNTs (CNTs-2 and CNTs-4) showed higher showed higher viability and less cytotoxicity against BCa cell under in vitro condition [105]. In study by Dong et al. [106] a controlled photothermal hydrogel-based Carbon nanotube drug delivery system was developed to deliver DOX under in vivo condition. The study proved that CNTs containing DOX incorporated in hydrogel will have sustained effect and controlled drug delivery under the influence of light irradiation. In theoretical approach of using molecular dynamics simulation to analyse the release of DOX coadsorbed on and in the CNTs as a drug delivery system under pH ranging between 5 and 7. This study stated that under acidic pH CNTs show spontaneous drug release against the tumour cells [107]. Carboxyl SWNTS modified with chitosan and bond with hyaluronan (target ligand for CD44) showed selective killing of cancer cells. This system exhibited sustained release of DOX at the acidic pH of 5.5 after 72 h. Also, this delivery system had lower cytotoxicity to fibroblast and did not damage the organs in rat model [108]. MWCNTs were functionalized with non-covalent bond to chitosan conjugated with trans-activator of transcription peptide. This was further tested under in vitro and in vivo condition showing efficient solubility in water and targeting the intracellular system of cancer cells [109]. Curcumin was functionalized on SWCNTs using polysaccharides like chitosan and alginate to demonstrate the efficacy of drug release using human lung adenocarcinoma cells. In a recent study, functionalized SWCNTs and MWCNTs were reacted with pyrrole derived compounds for the formation of DOX stacked CNTs delivery system. This system showed efficient delivery of DOX in the cell line under in vitro condition [110]. Pennetta et al. [110] reported the grafting of polyampholytes on SWCNTs with furfuryl amine using ultrasound and loaded with DOX. This delivery system showed high drug loading capacity and indicated the delivery of DOX under pH response showing high toxicity to HeLa cancer cell line. In a recent report anticancer drug Dacarbazine were function on the outer and inner surface of CNTs and showed efficient drug release in aqueous solution [111]. Therefore, CNTs suggested to be a potential drug delivery agent which can be functionalized with different polymers, ligands, and lipids to efficiently delivery drug in response to temperature, pH and Light irradiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots for cancer diagnosis and treatment
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are small nanoparticles, crystalline or amorphous material with different surface passivation done by modification and functionalization methods. CQDs possess potential catalytic and physiochemical property making them suitable for biomedical application. They are smaller in size, biocompatible, greater option for drug delivery, easily trackable, less toxic, hydrophilic, water soluble and chemically stable [112]. To demonstrate the efficacy of CQDs against cancer cells research published valuable research work demonstrating their potential role as drug delivery agent ( Fig. 6).

A new class of multifunctional fluorescent CQDs were synthesized using one pot hydrothermal reaction of L-systeine and m-phenylenediamine. The CDS were conjugated with photosensitizer namely protoporphyrin IX, this increased the cellular uptake and targeted the nucleolus of the cancer cells. CQDs injected via intravenous injection under in vivo condition resulted in the tumor ablation after irradiation with laser, also the CQDs were non-toxic to the normal cells [113]. QDs were conjugated in multiplex stacks for cellular and molecular mapping of human cancer cells. CQDs synthesized using nitric acid treatment of high temperature and carbon soot with PEG showed biocompatible and competitive bioimaging then CDSe/ZnS QDs [76]. A Nano-theranostic platform was developed based on CQDs dope with S, N, Gd and functionalised with folic acid to develop a magnetic and fluorescent properties. Under in vitro the developed CQDs showed low toxicity and high biocompatibility, also enhancing the magnetic resonance response and targeted the HepG2 and HeLa cancer cells [114]. Sodium alginates were used for the synthesis CQDs were used for gene delivery. On step hydrothermal carbonization methods were used for the synthesis of CQDs using alginate as carbon source and cationization agents. The CQDs synthesized showed efficient condensation of plasmid DNA and showed high transfection efficiency to play a major role as gene vectors and as bioimaging probes [115]. Sui et al. [116] synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) to propose the mechanism of targeted drug delivery and enhanced uptake of anticancer drug namely cisplatin. Magneto fluorescent carbon quantum dots were conjugated with folic acid and riboflavin. This conjugation of CQDs were light triggered for application in photothermal and photodynamic therapy. In vitro and in vivo experiments revealed the efficacy of CQDs for intracellular delivery of anticancer drugs within the tumour cells using NIR irradiation [10]. CQDs covalently linked with DOX showed 62.8 % of drug loading capacity with increase water dispersibility. Labelling fluorescents ensured the tracking of CQDs in the human breast cancer cells. Under in vivo condition CQDs loaded with DOX showed efficient inhibition of tumour cells with lesser cytotoxicity and side effects [98]. Red emissive CQDs loaded with DOX decreased the cell viability of HeLa cells to 21 % showing excelled therapeutic effect for elimination of cancer stem cells [117]. DOX conjugated pH responsive CQDs were tested against cancer cells showed 65 % drug release and reduction in tumor cells in response to acidic pH (5.0) [118]. In a study by Li et al. [119], mitochondria were used as aircraft for CQDs to deliver DOX against cancer cells. CQDs showed efficient therapeutic effect against cancer cells and increased the drug release rate after intravenous injection. CQDs conjugated with chitosan and loaded with 5-fluorouracil showed controlled drug release and killing of tumor in MCF-7 cells. This study suggested the potential of 5-fluorouracil-chitosan-CQDs against breast cancer [120]. In another reports, Black phosphorus quantum dots (BPQDs) were systematically studied in interaction with GOD, and the dose-dependent conformational and enzymatic activity changes of BPQDs on GOD were quantitatively and qualitatively analysed. It seems like BPQDs, and GOD are roughly stoichiometric. The changes in secondary and tertiary conformations of GOD induced by BPQDs were studied using fluorescence spectroscopy, circular dichroism spectra, and Fourier transforms infrared spectra. As BPQD doses were increased, a looser structure of GOD developed, though it retained its native conformation and effective enzymatic activity that catalyses the formation of H2O2 from glucose. Using biomass-derived GQDs and Stöber hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (hMSNs), Raegen et al. [121] developed graphene quantum dots-hollow mesoporous silica nanoparticles (GQDs-hMSNs) for fluorescent imaging and dual treatment of cancer via drug delivery and photodynamic therapy. Despite the higher toxicity of GQDs-hMSNs at higher concentrations, GQDs-hMSNs demonstrated excellent drug delivery with FITC and PDT treatments with GQDs [121]. Water solubility, biocompatibility, simplicity of synthesis, and low cytotoxicity of CQDs derived from natural sources make them attractive for medical imaging. This study demonstrated enhanced cytotoxicity of ethylene-diamine-doped CQDs. Results showed that treated cells had a higher percentage of apoptotic and necrotic cells than untreated cells, 19.7 × 0.03 % and 27.6 × 0.06 %, respectively. Furthermore, untreated cells showed a more substantial reduction in cell numbers in the S phase (18 % versus 29 %). Comparing N-CQDs and FA-NCQDs to untreated cells (0.34 + 0.04 ng/mL), caspase-3 levels were significantly increased in A549 exposed to N-CQDs. Therefore, CQDs showed greater potential as drug delivery agent for the treatment and diagnosis of cancer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (F-CNPs) for cancer cell imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles (F-CNPs) are an excellent technological alternative since they are highly water soluble, flexible when modified with chemicals, biocompatible, have good cell permeability, and highly photostable [122]. The fluorescent CNPs have a size of 2–6 nm and can be synthesized by oxidizing carbon soot with nitric acid [122]. F-CNPs are composed of only carbon with intrinsic fluorescence properties, so they should have low toxicity. Depending on their synthesis, these particles may contain different functional groups on their surface, e.g., -COOH, -OH, >CO, and -NH2, which confers excellent water solubility and opportunities for covalent conjugation with chemotherapeutics, targeting agents, and antibodies [60,123]. In addition to being a nontoxic drug delivery system, F-CNPs could also be used in bioimaging. The fluorescence of CNMs can be increased by passivating the surface with polymer or ethylene glycol.

Bioimaging could be improved with carbon nanoparticles with red or NIR fluorescence to avoid background noise from endogenous fluorophores. In addition to providing valuable tools to medicinal chemists for designing site-specific carriers of various therapeutic agents, the development of highly biocompatible and fluorescent drug delivery systems using fluorescent carbon nanoparticles holds great promise for specific drug delivery with minimal side effects and toxicity in patients with cancer [60]. Wang et al. synthesized fluorescent carbon dots (F-CDs) of an average size of 2.5 nm. In the presence of 12.5 mg/mL F-CDs for 48 h, the cells showed no evident inhibition of viability [51]. Accordingly, F-CDs could be used for live cell imaging breast cancer cells under in vivo conditions. CD-Asp was synthesized using d-glucose and l-aspartic acidic as a starting material. The fluorescence images obtained after 15 min of CD-Asp injection in mouse model showed a high level of contrast in the biodistribution of the drug. The gliomas site showed stronger fluorescence that normal brain cells, thus indicating the ability of CD-Asp to penetrate the blood brain barriers. Therefore CD-Asp potential candidate for fluorescence imaging and getting of non-invasive diagnosis of gliomas [124]. Wang et al. [125] synthesized GQDs-AS1411 and tested the capability for specific tumor cell labeling and photothermal therapy. In response to excitation at 480 nm, AS1411-QGD solution and bare GQDs emit green fluorescence. AS1411–GQDs were incubated for 5 h with four tumor cell lines (A549, HeLa, MCF-7, and HepG-2) and two normal cell lines (COS-7 and HEK293). It was noted that there was no obvious fluorescence signal in either of the two normal cell lines, whereas the 4 tumor cell lines showed bright fluorescence signals with different values. Green fluorescent carbon quantum dots (GCQDs) with an average diameter of 3 nm were synthesized using a facile chemical slicing method. To enhance their targeting ability, the GCQDs were functionalized with folic acid (FA). Due to the abundance of folate receptors (FR) on various cancer cell membranes, FA-GCQDs positively influenced their binding potential and penetration into the cancer cells. MCF-7 breast cancer cells were also targeted in vitro with excellent photoluminescence stability, biocompatibility, and cytoplasm and nucleus targeting [126]. A nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot (N-CQD)-based sensor platform for detection of β-glucuronidase has been developed by Lu et al. [127] which offers new insights for the development of sensitive assays based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots. Nucleolin is targeted and bound by S1411, an oligodeoxynucleotide aptamer with 26 bases of guanine. Several cancer cells can be detected using CDs-AS1411aptamer, developed by Motaghi et al. [128]. To measure the fluorescence intensity signals, CDs-aptamer suspensions were incubated with target cancer cells, including mouse breast 4 T1, human breast MCF7, and human cervical HeLa cancer cells. In Yang et al. [129] peptide iRGDs are decorated onto red shift emissive CDs to create an active tumor targeting imaging system. The uptake of iRGD-CDs into cells and their toxicity were investigated in 4 T1 cells. CDs in tumor vessels and tumor tissue are more permeable when decorated with iRGD peptides. In turn, they make CDs more effective at targeting and imaging. A new class of green-emissive fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) has been used to selectively ablate cervical cancer cells (HeLa) over human dermal fibroblasts (HDF). With inexpensive and commercial starting materials, FCDs can easily be prepared in one pot. A thin surface coating of 2,5-deoxyfructosazine on a robust amorphous core was found to provide the nanomaterial's unique properties. Intracellular uptake is mostly passive, with DNA interactions targeting the nucleus, and cancer cell killing outcomes may be the result of ATP depletion and increased intracellular temperature, which cannot be seen with mere surface components or "naked" core FCDs [130]. One-step microwave hydrothermal carbonization of glycerol and PEI-25k was used to synthesize CD-PEIs loaded with DOX. DOX-loaded CD-PEI showed improved and selective drug release for a prolonged time and killing of liver cancer cells (MHCC-97 L and Hep3B) [131]. Using Taxus leaves as the raw material, Yang's group synthesized a NIR fluorescent CD peaking at 673 nm with a FLQY of 59 % (*ex = 413 nm) in DMSO. CDs were injected intravenously into C2C12 cells, causing strong red fluorescence in the cytoplasm. The fluorescence beA time-dependent decline in fluorescence was observed after 24 h, and fluorescence imaging was observed in each organ. This indicated that CDs can be excreted through the kidneys or liver [50]. A fluorescent and colorimetric probe based on carbon dots (CDs) that detects BPO on a visual, sensitive, and selective basis. By adding BPO, red fluorescence of CDs peaked at 622 and 677 nm can be quenched, while the absorbance at 613 nm is decreased and the absorbance at 450 nm is increased, resulting in colorimetric spectral response and fluorescence turn-off. Biomedical applications of F-CNM in cancer research have been opened by their exceptional properties like biocompatibility, good intracellular solubility, high cell permeability, photoluminescence, photostability, and water dispersibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogel based CNM for cancer therapy
ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogels have gained much attention in biomedical and pharmaceutical applications because they possess a biomimetic structure like the extracellular matrix of natural living tissues, tunable soft-porous microarchitectures, excellent biomechanical properties, and are biocompatibility-friendly [132]. Some hydrogels are made ex vivo as pre-shaped structures before being implanted in the body. A second option is injectable hydrogels that can be extruded through a needle or catheter because they are flowable. Due to this, they can be administered minimally invasively and do not require open surgery. Because they are comfortable, less painful, faster, cheaper, and have fewer complications and side effects, they are more attractive to patients and clinicians [133–135]. During injection, injectable hydrogels convert from a solution to a gel by converting to a suspension of particles. Covalent or non-covalent crosslinking occurs in situ to cause their gelation. When stimulus-responsive polymers are exposed to environmental factors, non-covalent physical bonds may form. The process can also be triggered by ionic bonding [136] or by the self-assembly of proteins [137]. Photo crosslinking or covalent precursors may occur in situ to create covalent bonds between the injected components, whereas covalent bonds are created by in situ biocompatible chemical reactions. A few examples of the latter case include enzymatic reactions [138,139], Michael addition reactions [140,141] and Schiff-base reactions [141].

A hydrogel/aerogel structure can be created by combining carbon composite structures with polymers [142]. The mechanical, electrical, and thermal properties of carbon-based nanomaterials such as graphene oxide, carbon nanotubes, and nanodiamonds make them interesting for biomedical applications [143]. Several external stimuli, including pH, temperature, chemical, and physicochemical, can control the therapeutic release of carbon-based structures [144]. Drugs can be transported by attaching drugs to graphene oxide (GO) and linking them with pH-sensitive linkers. Carbon-based aerogels offer higher porosity and lightweight properties in terms of porosity and specific surface area, while carbon-based aerogels offer higher porosity and lightweight properties. It is possible to create carbon aerogels from GO, which has intrinsic properties that make it an attractive biomaterial for cancer treatment and drug delivery. A 2-dimensional planar structure of GO with a hydrophilic and biocompatible surface allows molecules and functional groups to be incorporated [145]. GO aerogels supported with PVA proved more porous and stable than unsupported GO.

Wang and Chen [146], synthesized chitosan/β-glycerophosphate disodium salt (CS/GP) injectable hydrogels with smaller holes on the wall and tighter pores than neat CS/GP hydrogels. By forming hydrogen bonds between ATP nanoparticles and CS molecules, FT-IR tests revealed that ATP increased cross-link density [146]. A carbon graphene scaffold with functionalized Multiwalled Carbon Nanotubes (f-MWCNT), chitosan, and glycerophosphate was fabricated and characterized. F-MWCNTs have indeed improved electrical conductivity and mechanical properties when their content was increased. Still, biodegradability and mechanical tests suggest these increases have some limitations, such as a decrease in mechanical properties and biodegradability when f-MWCNTs are present at 1 w/v%. MTT was also used to test the scaffolds for cytotoxicity, but no significant results were found [147]. It has been shown that a thermal-sensitive hydrogel based on chitosan (CS) and glycerophosphate (GP) can degrade quickly and biodegrade effectively. Three weight ratios of GO/CS were used to fabricate CS/GP-based hydrogels (0.5, 1, 2 %). As a result of these assessments, it was found that GO added to CS/GP improved the properties of the hydrogels without affecting their microstructure and swelling ability. When small amounts of GO (0.5 % weight ratio of CS) were added, the gelation time at body temperature was significantly reduced by nearly 20 %. Compared to CS/GP hydrogels, hydrogels containing GO demonstrated greatly improved mechanical properties. At gelling temperature, hydrogels with GO had storage/loss moduli 1.12–1.69 times greater than those with CS/GP. Compared to CS/GP hydrogel, 0.5 %GO/CS/GP hydrogel has Young's modulus of 1.76 times. As well as demonstrating remarkable biological affinity, the 0.5 %GO/CS/GP hydrogel showed an increase in viability and proliferation when cells were attached to the gel [148].

To develop a prolonged release, a pH-responsive chitosan (CS)- montmorillonite (MMT)-nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) nanocomposite was formed, loaded with DOX, and then incorporated into a double emulsion [149]. Loading and entrapping NCQDs in CS-MMT hydrogels was more effortless after NCQDs were included. Compared to CS-MMT-DOX nanocarriers at pH 5.4, NCQD nanoparticles in the CS-MMT hydrogel prolonged DOX release for 96 h. Korsmeyer-Peppas showed that DOX released at pH 5.4 was controlled but not at pH 7.4, indicating fewer side effects [149]. MCF-7 cells treated with DOX-loaded CS-MMT-NCQD hydrogel nanocomposite showed significantly higher cytotoxicity than cells treated with free DOX. The induction of apoptosis by NCQD nanoparticles in CS-MMT-DOX nanocarriers was higher [149]. A hydrogel injectable with bioadhesion properties was developed by He et al. [150] to provide immunomodulation and catalytic immunotherapy in tumor microenvironments. Using catachol-grafted carbon-quantum-dot templates (DA-CQD@Pd), in situ synthesis synthesized a phenolic single-atom nano-enzyme (SAN) in the first step [150]. A SAN-catalyzed free-radical polymerization was carried out to form an injectable bio adhesive hydrogel with DA-CQD@Pd SAN and CpGODN. The catalytic therapy generated ROS, such as peroxidase. CpGODN was released locally and sustainably using hydrogel, so it wasn't systemically exposed, the toxicity of CpGODN was reduced, and degradation of CpGODN was prevented [150].

A polymer can be integral to delivering therapeutic agents over extended periods in constant dosages, cyclic dosages, and tunable release of both hydrophilic and hydrophobic drugs. They offer a chemical degradation barrier and some nanoparticulate polymeric systems can cross the blood-brain barrier. The responsive properties of intelligent polymers, such as their ability to respond to atmospheric stimuli, such as temperature changes, pressure changes, pH changes, etc., make them ideal for delivering drugs in targeted ways [151]. Conjugating certain polymeric systems with antibodies and specific biomarkers allow cancer-specific molecular target detection. The water solubility and photostability of surfaces coated with thiolated PEG and Silica-PEG are improved [151]. Drug carriers can also circulate more quickly when their surface is modified, e.g., by attaching PEG to the lipid bilayer or dextran to the lipid bilayer. Treatments for prostate and lymphoblastic leukemia cancers include polymer-drug conjugate therapies such as Zoladex, Lupron Depot, and On Caspar PEG intron. The use of biodegradable polymers in drug delivery has gained widespread traction because they can degrade inside the body into non-toxic monomers [151].

PLGA-coated CNMs showed high loading efficiency for paclitaxel and demonstrated antitumor activity against cancer cells in vitro [152–154]. Researchers found DOX nanocarriers based on SWCNTs derivatized with carboxylates and coated with polysaccharides showed significant efficacy against cancer models [155,156]. Researchers have developed a new nanomaterial that can be combined with checkpoint-blockade immunotherapy for improved cancer treatment [157]. In detail, the immunoadjuvant nanoagents (γ-PGA@GOx@M n, Cu-CDs) were fabricated by integrating the gamma-glutamyl transferase (GGT) enzyme-induced cellular uptake polymer-poly (γ-PGA), glucose oxidase (GOx), Mn, Cu-doped carbon dots (CDs) as photosensitizers and self-supplied oxygenator nanodots. Cancer cells and tumor acidic environments could retain these nanoparticles (NPs) for long periods. Photothermal and photodynamic effects were also induced by laser irradiation at 730 nm [157]. In vivo PDT was enhanced by the endogenous hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) generation caused by nanoreactors. Treatment efficiency was improved significantly through a synergistic combination of NPs-based starving-like therapy/PDT/PTT and check-point-blockade therapy [157]. Zhang and Gauthier developed a method for fabricating carbon nanotube (CNT) foam [158]. This composite contains controllable cell shapes and distributions, so the properties are tuneable, like density, porosity, elasticity, conductivity, and strength.

The production and biomedical application of CNMs increase the risk of occupational exposure. Thus, CNMs are becoming more likely to co-expose with environmental pollutants. There have been many studies on the combined toxicity of CNMs and pollutants. However, less attention is paid to the toxicity of nanomaterials after adsorbing pollutants or the toxicity of nanomaterials and pollutants exposed simultaneously. The body excretes compounds from its circulation, which helps regulate the composition of body tissues and fluids and reduces toxicity. There are two main routes for getting rid of nanoparticles, including CDs, through the kidney (urine) and liver (bile to feces). In addition to breast milk, tears, saliva, sweat, and the lungs, there are other pathways to get rid of them [159]. Within hours to days of intravenous administration, particles are primarily excreted through the kidneys. On the other hand, the hepatic pathway can leave particles in the body for a long time (months to years) [159]. The NPs can process particles that don't degrade. Therefore, they can cause immune responses and stay inside your body for months to years if the liver or renal systems can't process them. Prolonged body retention can cause chronic toxicity and inflammation. For nanotherapeutics to be commercially viable, they must be excreted from the body after use [159].

In 2022, Kong et al. used THP-1 cells to investigate how pristine SWCNTs and oxidized SWCNT-COOHs pretreatments at a non-lethal dose of 10 μg/mL affect cell responses to metal ions (i. e., Pb2+, Cu2+, and Cr(VI)) [160]. This study reported that p-SWCNTs caused more significant damage to cell membrane integrity than SWCNT-COOHs, which led to higher metallothionein (MT) levels and increased transport of metal ions into cells. Pretreatment of p-SWCNTs in cells significantly increased the cytotoxicity of Pb2+, Cu2+, and Cr(VI) by 2–4-fold. In another study, the effects of multi-walled carbon nanotubes and copper oxide nanoparticles on Tetradesmus obliquus [161]. NPs-affected algae and MWCNTs had a more substantial impact than CuO NPs, with median effective concentrations of 33.8 mg/L and 169.2 mg/L, respectively, over 96 h [161]. There was a concentration-dependent relationship between oxidative stress and cell membrane damage for NP toxicity to algae. CuO nanoparticles in some groups reduced cell membrane damage caused by MWCNTs. It's possible because CuO NPs form larger aggregations with MWCNTs, reducing contact between the nanoparticles and the cell membrane and reducing physical damage [161]. Zhao et al. [162] showed that germline long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs) mediated transgenerational MWCNT toxicity in Caenorhabditis elegans. MWCNT exposure resulted in transgenerational toxicity measured by brood size and locomotion behaviour. Therefore, these studies clearly suggested the harmful effect of CNMs on mammalian cells, environment, and aquatic organisms. The toxicity of CNMs can be reduced by choosing the right material and method for synthesis. In addition, functionalization, layer, foaming and hydrogel formation improves the toxicity of CNMs.

Among the growing concerns in the developing world, cancer has become a major cause for death. To solve the issue with diagnosis and treatment of cancer, carbon nanoparticle stands in the forefront as a powerful drug delivery agent. Different types of CNMs can been used for cancer treatment and diagnosis but considering the toxicity and solubility so far only graphene sheets, carbon quantum dots and carbon nanotubes have been successfully used. These CNMs are less toxic than other metallic nanoparticles, also they can be functionalized with different ligands to conjugate anti-cancer drugs in or on their structure. Different polymer like PEG, chitosan has been used for the functionalization with ligand specific sites that can easily bind to the tumour cells. Growing concern over the removal or toxicity of CNMs has been solved by using organic materials for the synthesis of CNMs. Further continuing the research towards improving the target specificity and drug loading capacity will give fruitful solution to develop prolonged treatment strategies. In recent time CNMs loaded with siRNA has proved to be effective in targeting the cancer cells. Developing and identifying new anti-cancer drugs or agents will help in producing more effective delivery systems like CQDs. Also loading dual drugs in a single CNMS will help in providing 100 % efficacy towards cancer cells. Future studies should examine how nucleation/growth processes affect structure/properties, how to control their morphology/size, and how to find photoluminescence mechanisms. A crucial factor that must be addressed in the future is the reproducibility of synthesis techniques. Thus, continuing more research on different shapes and structure of CNMs will provide further ideas towards continuing the fight against cancer.

Kathirvel Brindhadevi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Hakim AL Garalleh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Amr Alalawi: Writing – original draft. Ebtisam Al-Sarayreh: Writing – review & editing. Arivalagan Pugazhendhi: Supervision, Project administration, Reviewing and Editing.
